coredata.dc:title: Industrial stainless steel meshes for efficient electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yanhong
Zhang, Kewei
Zhang, Dongxu
Dong, Weixuan
Jiang, Tianyao
Zhou, Hongbo
Li, Lixia
Mao, Baodong
coredata.dc:description: The electrocatalytic water splitting to hydrogen and oxygen plays a critical role on renewable energy conversion and storage, which relies heavily on the development of high-efficiency, low-cost and robust catalysts. Here, employing the industrial 316 L stainless steel (SS), the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) performance of a series of SS meshes with different mesh number (100–1600) were studied in acidic condition. The results exhibit that the cheap industrial 316 L SS meshes have excellent electrocatalytic HER activity. Meanwhile, the electrocatalytic HER performance of the 316 L SS meshes increases with the increase of mesh number. The HER activity was increased with the mesh number increases from 100 to 1600 meshes, where the optimized 316 L SS-1600 obtains a low overpotential of 209.8 mV at the current density of 10 mA cm−2 and a small Tafel slope (115.6 mV dec−1). However, with further increase of the mesh number from 1600 meshes to 2800 meshes, the HER performance started to decrease. A possible mechanism for the effect of the mesh number on HER performance was proposed, which can be ascribed to the electrochemical specific surface area and the tight interweaving of SS mesh for hydrogen release. This work provides an important direction for the performance improvement of the low-cost industrially available electrodes towards scale-up electrocatalytic hydrogen production and renewable energy storage.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 316 L stainless steel
Mesh numbers
Hydrogen evolution reaction
Hydrogen production
Electrolysis
Transition metals

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: EDS of 316 L SS-1600(a) and 316L SS-2800(b).
Acidic HER LSV curves (a), bar chart of overpotentials (b), corresponding Tafel curves (c) and the bar chart of Tafel slopes (d) for different SS meshes.
Acidic HER polarization curves (LSV) (a) and Tafel slopes (b) for 316 L SS-1600, 304 SS-1600 and Pt/C.
(a) EIS of SS with different mesh numbers and (b) the corresponding model of equivalent circuits.
CV curves of 316 L SS with mesh numbers of 100 (a), 500 (b), 1000 (c), 1600 (d), 2000 (e), and 2800 (f).
Estimation of the electrochemically active surface area from the capacitance current versus scan rate for 316 L SS with different mesh sizes at 0.15 V vs. RHE (Δj at 0.15 V).
Stability test of 1600 mesh 316 L SS after CV 2000 cycles.
Atomic ratio contained in 316 L SS-1600 and 316 L SS-2800.
Summary of HER performance of the SS meshes in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution.

head.ce:title: Industrial stainless steel meshes for efficient electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Green Chemistry & Chemical Technology, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Green Chemistry & Chemical Technology
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aInstitute of Green Chemistry & Chemical Technology, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of the Environment and Safety Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of the Environment and Safety Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bSchool of the Environment and Safety Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The electrocatalytic water splitting to hydrogen and oxygen plays a critical role on renewable energy conversion and storage, which relies heavily on the development of high-efficiency, low-cost and robust catalysts. Here, employing the industrial 316 L stainless steel (SS), the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) performance of a series of SS meshes with different mesh number (100–1600) were studied in acidic condition. The results exhibit that the cheap industrial 316 L SS meshes have excellent electrocatalytic HER activity. Meanwhile, the electrocatalytic HER performance of the 316 L SS meshes increases with the increase of mesh number. The HER activity was increased with the mesh number increases from 100 to 1600 meshes, where the optimized 316 L SS-1600 obtains a low overpotential of 209.8 mV at the current density of 10 mA cm−2 and a small Tafel slope (115.6 mV dec−1). However, with further increase of the mesh number from 1600 meshes to 2800 meshes, the HER performance started to decrease. A possible mechanism for the effect of the mesh number on HER performance was proposed, which can be ascribed to the electrochemical specific surface area and the tight interweaving of SS mesh for hydrogen release. This work provides an important direction for the performance improvement of the low-cost industrially available electrodes towards scale-up electrocatalytic hydrogen production and renewable energy storage.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen production from water electrolysis is one of the simplest, environmentally friendly, and feasible ways to provide high-quality hydrogen, but also plays a crucial role in converting excessive renewable electricity into chemical energy and storage it in hydrogen as a clean energy carrier [1–3]. However, in the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) process of water electrolysis, it often unnecessarily consumes a large amount of electric energy due to the high overpotential if there is no effective electrocatalyst [4–6]. Therefore, designing electrocatalysts with high performance has become a hot spot in current research to reduce overpotential [7–9]. Although Pt-based noble metal materials have shown high HER activity, these materials are difficult to be widely used and promoted in industrial electrocatalytic water splitting due to their extremely scariness and high price [10–12]. At present, exploring for cheap and efficient electrocatalysts is a major concern in the development of industrial application of electrocatalytic HER [13].

Transition metal materials have been extensively studied for HER, where Fe, Ni and Mo-based materials have been proven to be among the most effective class of electrocatalysts [14–17]. Meanwhile, there are a lot of work to dope or compound these metal materials to further improve HER activity of the electrocatalysts [10, 18–21]. As an alloy material with high acid resistance, alkali resistance, high temperature resistance and high physical strength, stainless steel (SS) is widely used in the industry. At the same time, SS has been used as an aqueous lithium battery electrode in saturated LiOH solution due to its low price and good stability [22–24]. In addition, research has also preliminarily shown that it also has great application potential in water electrolysis [25,26]. For example, SS meshes contain iron, nickel and molybdenum and other metal elements that can be used as candidates for HER or oxygen evolution reaction (OER) [27–29]. Schäfer et al. reported that 304 SS has excellent OER performance [30]. Sun et al. found that the commercial 316 L SS (containing Fe, Ni, Cr, Mo) has high OER catalytic activity [31]. Castellanos et al. studied different types of SS in the industry, which have excellent alkaline HER activity [32]. Although these works can well prove the application potential of SS in water electrolysis, the researches lack depth and the excavation of the biggest advantage of SS itself, e.g. industrially easy to achieve structural design and construction, and subsequently on electrocatalytic HER performance.

In this work, different from common chemical methods, here we propose a simple industrially applicable method for tuning the HER activity of SS by directly using 316 L SS of different mesh numbers (100, 500, 1000, 1600, 2000 and 2800). The experimental results show that when the number of 316 L SS mesh increases from 500 to 1000 and 1600, the catalytic HER activity of SS was continuously improved. The 1600 mesh 316 L SS (316 L SS-1600) exhibits the best electrocatalytic HER performance, which obtains the small overpotential of 209.8 mV at the current density of 10 mA cm−2. However, when the mesh number was further increased to 2000 and 2800, the electrocatalytic HER performance decreased. Through the experimental tests, it was found that the microstructure and wire diameter of different 316 L SS meshes are very different, resulting in different electrocatalytic HER activities. This study provides an important example for the promotion and direct application of pure SS in electrocatalysis, beyond acting as the substrate for growing electrocatalysts.

Absolute ethanol was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent. Sulfuric acid (H2SO4) was bought from Tianjin Chemical Corporation. The SS meshes with various mesh numbers were obtained from the market (Shanghai Xintai Wire Mesh Co., Ltd). Deionized water was used during the whole experiment process. All the reagents were used directly without further purification in our experiments.

First, the 316 L SS meshes of different mesh numbers (100, 500, 1000, 1600, 2000 and 2800 mesh) were cut to the size of 0.5 × 0.5 cm2 for cleaning. The cut SS meshes were then ultrasonically treated with 1 M HCl, ethanol and deionized water for 30 min to avoid the influence of impurities on the surface of the samples, which will be used directly as the working electrodes in further study.

The crystal structure of the SS samples were confirmed by X-ray diffraction (XRD) using a Remake RINT 2000 X-ray diffractometer (Bruker AXS company, Germany) with a Cu-Kα (λ = 1.542 Å) radiation source in the 2θ range of 20–80°at a scanning rate of 5.0° min−1. The morphology and composition of the catalysts were analyzed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) using a Hitachi S-4800 microscope, operated at an accelerating voltage of 15 kV.

The electrochemical performance of the 316 L SS meshes was measured using a CHI 760E electrochemical workstation. The entire test procedure was maintained at room temperature using a typical three-electrode system in 0.5 M H2SO4 (pH = 0.4) with saturated N2. The counter electrode was a graphite rod, a piece of 316 L SS mesh of 0.5 × 0.5 cm2 was used as the working electrode and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) acts as the reference electrode. The tested data was then converted to the values vs. reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) according to the Nernst formula: E = E (SCE) + 0.2415 + 0.0596 pH.

The linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) measurement was used to reflect the HER performance of the 316 L SS with the scan rate of 5 mV s − 1 at the overpotential range of −0.7 - 0 V vs. RHE, for which a cyclovoltaic (CV) activation of the SS meshes is required at the beginning of the test. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests were performed at a low frequency of 0.01 Hz and a high frequency of 1000 KHz at an overpotential of 0.35 mV. The electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) was estimated from the capacitance current vs. scan rate for the SS meshes at 0.15 V vs. RHE (Δj = 0.15 V). The stability of 1600 mesh 316 L SS was tested with 2000 CV cycles.

The lattice structure of 316 L SS-1600 was studied by XRD. As shown in Fig. 1 , the XRD pattern of 316 L SS shows the same characteristic peaks as austenite, which is called the γ phase. The diffraction peaks of 2θ at 43.8°, 50.9° and 74.8° matched with the (111), (200) and (220) crystal faces of 316 L SS (PDF#33–0397), respectively [33].

The morphology of 316 L SS with different mesh numbers was analyzed by SEM. Various SS wires were woven together to form SS meshes. It can be observed from Fig. 2 that the wire diameters of the different SS meshes are different. The 100-mesh SS has a wire diameter of approximately 94 µm (Fig. 2a), the 500-mesh wire diameter is about 29 µm (Fig. 2b), the 2000-mesh wire diameter is about 39 µm (Fig. 2e), and the 2800-mesh diameter is about 24 µm (Fig. 2f). However, there are two kinds of wire diameters in 1000-mesh (66 µm and 45 µm, Fig. 2c) and 1600-mesh SS (66 µm and 51 µm, Fig. 2d) due to the different weaving requirement. The 1000-mesh and 1600-mesh SS are composed of a thick wire and a thin wire through a "mat" weaving. The 100-mesh and 500-mesh SS are simply braided together using a mesh of the same wire diameter. In addition, 2000 mesh and 2800 mesh SS are woven together through a "twisted" configuration. Therefore, the effective catalytic activive area provided by the mesh, the wire diameter and the configuration of the SS might also be different.

At the same time, the composition and element content of SS were analyzed by EDS measurement. EDS test was performed on 316 L SS-1600 (Fig. 3 a) and 316 L SS-2800 (Fig. 3b). Through EDS, it can be found that 316 L SS is mainly composed of Fe, Ni, Cr, Mo and C elements. However, there are only slight differences in the element contents of SS in different meshes, as summarized in Table 1 . The atomic ratios of Fe:Ni:Cr:Mo are 1: 0.20: 0.30: 0.03 and 1: 0.27: 0.26: 0.02 in 316 L SS-1600 and 316 L SS-2800, respectively. By comparison, the percentage of each element in the SS can be found, and the percentage of the metal elements in different samples are very close, especially the HER active Ni and Mo elements. The difference in the content of these SS elements would not result in a big difference in the electrocatalytic active sites.

The HER performance of different SS meshes was tested in a 0.5 M H2SO4 (pH=0.4) electrolyte. Fig. 4 a and Fig. 4b show the LSV curves and overpotentials of 316 L SS with different mesh numbers, respectively. Through LSV test and overpotentials, it can be found that when the 316 L SS mesh number was increased from 100 to 1600, the HER activity of SS was continuously improved, as summarized in Table 2 . 316 L SS-1600 exhibits the best HER performance, and obtains overpotentials of 209.8 and 314.7 mV at the current density of 10 and 50 mA cm−2, respectively. However, when the mesh number of SS further increases from 1600 to 2800, the electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity decreases. This trend of HER performance may be due to the surface area and exposed active sites in the 316 L SS with different mesh numbers. If the mesh number of 316 L SS is too low, such as 316 L SS-100, it will not provide a large catalytic active area. However, when the mesh number is too high, such as 316 L SS-2800, the SS wires in the meshes will be closely overlapped in the twisting weaving. Although the catalytic active area is relatively large, the effective catalytic active sites are few, and the generated hydrogen may not be released in time, which affects the activity of the SS meshes [4].

The Tafel curves (Fig. 4c) of the different 316 L SS meshes were further obtained by transforming the LSV data in Fig. 4a. As summarized in Fig. 4d, 316 L SS-1600 has the lowest Tafel slope of 115.6 mV dec−1, while the Tafel slopes of both 316 L SS-100 (136.6 mV dec−1) and 316 L SS-2800 (118 mV dec−1) are larger than 316 L SS-1600. It worth to note that the Tafel slopes of different SS meshes matched well with their HER overpotentials, indicating that 316 L SS-1600 possesses rapid HER kinetics [34, 35]. And the HER performance of various samples is summarized in Table 2.

In addition, another more commonly used SS mesh with the same mesh number but different composition, 304 SS-1600, was also tested for HER to compare with 316 L SS-1600. As shown in Fig. 5 , the HER activity of 316 L SS-1600 is significantly better than 304 SS, with overpotentials of 209.8 mV and 255.4 mV at 10 mA cm−2, respectively, which is also superior than uncoated SS in the literature [4]. At the current density of 10 mA cm−2, the overpotential of 316 L SS-1600 is 45 mV lower than that of 304 SS-1600. And the Tafel slope of 304 SS-1600 (120.9 mV dec−1) is also slightly larger than that of 316 L SS-1600 (115.6 mV dec−1), indicating that the electrocatalytic HER activity of 304 L SS of the same mesh number is lower than 316 L SS. In addition, the LSV curve and overpotential of Pt/C are also given in Fig. 5a and 5b for comparision. The difference in electrocatalytic performance of the two identical SS meshes may be attributed to the fact that the 304 L SS and 316 L SS contain different metal elements and the percentages of the components. 316 L SS is mainly composed of Fe, Ni, Cr and Mo, whereas 304 SS has less Ni and more Cr. It can be seen that the industrial 316 L SS shows superior HER performance than 304 SS, and is more suitable as potential electrode candidates for electrocatalytic hydrogen production.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is an important approach to evaluate the speed of charge transfer between the electrode/electrolyte interface during the HER process [36–38]. Fig. 6 a shows the EIS of SS with different mesh numbers. The impedance of 316 L SS-1600 has the smallest semi-arc. The equivalent circuit simulation of EIS of different SS meshes is shown in Fig. 6b, from which the Rct values were obtained. The Rct value of the 316 L SS-1600 is 28.8 Ω, lower than those of 316 L SS-100 (305.2 Ω), 316 L SS-500 (161.3 Ω), 316 L SS-1000 (94.5 Ω), 316 L SS-2000 (54.4 Ω), 316 L SS-2800 (33.5 Ω), as well as 304 L SS-1600 (61.6 Ω). EIS tests have shown that 316 L SS-1600 has faster HER kinetics, consistent with the HER activity results in Fig. 4 [39].

To further investigate the electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) of different 316 L SS meshes, the slopes obtained by CV measurement and linear fitting was used as the electric double layer capacitance (Cdl) value. And the converted Cdl value can be used to evaluate the intrinsic catalytically active surface area of the catalysts [40–42]. As shown in Fig. 7 , the CV tests for different SS meshes were performed in the non-polarized region of 0.1–0.2 V vs. RHE with the scan rates of 10, 20, 40, 80, 120, 160 and 200 mV s − 1 in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution. Fig. 8 shows the plots of capacitance current at 0.15 V (vs. RHE) vs. scan rates, and the linear fitting slope is considered to be an effective method for estimating Cd1 [43–45]. As a result, Cdl of 316 L SS-1600 is 2.01 mF cm−2, which is much higher than 316 L SS-100 (0.73 mF cm−2), 316 L SS-500 (0.87 mF cm−2), 316 L SS-1000 (1.01 mF cm−2), 316 L SS-2000 (1.14 mF cm−2) and 316 L SS-2800 (1.18 mF cm−2), indicating the larger ECSA of 316LSS-1600, consistent with its superior HER activity [46].

Stability test is another important parameter for evaluating the performance of an electrocatalyst [38, 47-50]. Usually, SS cannot last long in acidic environment [51,52]. In contrast to the literature results, our 316 L SS meshes actually show good long-term stability for HER. This can be attributed to the fact the 316 L SS meshes were industrially produced with high quality. Here, as shown in Fig. 9 , the stability of the 316 L SS-1600 is reflected by comparing the LSV curves of the sample before and after 2000 cycles of CV tests with a scan rate of 100 mV s − 1. It can be seen that, after 2000 cycles, the LSV curve of 316 L SS-1600 substantially coincides with the initial LSV curve, indicating good stability under acidic condition, which can be seen that the activity of 316 L SS-1600 did not show any obvious change. The good stability along with the industrial availability render great potential of future practical application of these cheap SS meshes for hydrogen production.

In summary, here we conducted acidic HER on a series of industrial 316 L SS meshes. The activity of these 316 L SS meshes was tuned by the mesh number. The experimental results show that when the mesh number of 316 L SS was increased from 100 to 1600, the electrocatalytic HER performance was continuously improved. However, with further increase from 1600 mesh to 2800 mesh, the electrocatalytic HER activity was lowered. It was found that the optimized 316 L SS-1600 showed the best electrocatalytic HER activity, and the overpotentials of 209.8 and 314.7 mV were obtained at the current densities of 10 and 50 mA cm−2, respectively. 316 L SS-1600 has also shown an excellent stability. Further electrochemical measurements indicated that the improvement of electrocatalytic performance may result from the difference in wire diameter and weaving method of the SS meshes, resulting in different effective catalytic active areas and electrocatalytic active sites. This work provides a new strategy for the development of inexpensive, high performance HER catalysts from cheap SS meshes, especially the activity manipulation via industrial weaving process, which is of great interest for future scale applications of hydrogen production and renewable energy storage.

Yanhong Liu: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition. Kewei Zhang: Data curation, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Dongxu Zhang: Data curation, Visualization. Weixuan Dong: Data curation. Tianyao Jiang: Data curation. Hongbo Zhou: Formal analysis, Supervision. Lixia Li: Formal analysis, Supervision. Baodong Mao: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no Conflict of Interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Our work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21908081 and 21501072).
